title: Trial by river ordeal in the Code of Hammurabi
blurb: Hammurabi's law let the river judge the accused sorcerer: sink and you are guilty, float and you are cleared. Magic and law entangled from the first written codes.
source: Jean Bottéro, Mesopotamia

title: Empedocles and the four elements
blurb: Empedocles named the four 'roots' — earth, water, air, fire — bound by Love and Strife. The scheme underlies all later alchemy, to which Aristotle would add the fifth essence.
mtg: The elemental logic beneath Magic's five colours.
source: Lyndy Abraham, A Dictionary of Alchemical Imagery

title: The Sefer Yetzirah — creation by letters
blurb: The 'Book of Formation' teaches that God made the world through the twenty-two Hebrew letters and ten sefirot — the seed of Kabbalah and of all letter-magic.
source: Gershom Scholem, Kabbalah; RenaissanceMagicDB, s.v. sefer yetzirah

title: Stephanus of Alexandria and Byzantine alchemy
blurb: As Greco-Egyptian alchemy passed into Byzantium, Stephanus lectured on the 'divine art' at the imperial court, keeping the tradition alive for its later Arabic translators.
source: Principe, The Secrets of Alchemy

title: The Fourth Lateran Council hardens the pact
blurb: By defining heresy and the Devil ever more sharply, the thirteenth-century Church built the theological scaffolding on which magic would later be prosecuted as diabolism.
source: Bailey, The Sacred and the Sinister

title: The Sefer ha-Bahir surfaces in Provence
blurb: The 'Book of Brightness,' the first true Kabbalistic text, appeared in southern France, launching the medieval flowering of Jewish mysticism.
source: Scholem, Kabbalah

title: The Zohar
blurb: Moses de León composed (or transmitted) the Zohar, the towering work of Kabbalah, whose doctrine of divine names and emanations Renaissance Christians would later raid for magic.
source: Scholem, Kabbalah; RenaissanceMagicDB, s.v. sefer ha-zohar

title: Ramon Llull's combinatorial Art
blurb: Llull built rotating wheels of concepts to generate all truths mechanically — an 'Art' that fascinated later magicians and cryptographers, and that alchemists borrowed his name to sell.
source: Frances Yates, The Art of Memory

title: Cardano's De Subtilitate
blurb: The physician-mathematician Gerolamo Cardano systematised 'subtle' natural magic and the occult virtues of things, straddling magic and the new science.
source: William Eamon, Science and the Secrets of Nature (alchemy corpus)

title: Bodin's De la Démonomanie des sorciers
blurb: The great political theorist Jean Bodin also wrote a ferocious witch-hunting handbook, lending intellectual weight to the trials — a reminder that the age's best minds prosecuted magic.
source: Stuart Clark, Thinking with Demons (renaissance magic corpus)

title: Guazzo's Compendium Maleficarum
blurb: Guazzo's illustrated manual codified the sabbat, the pact, and the powers of the witch — its woodcuts fixing the popular image of maleficium for centuries.
source: Clark, Thinking with Demons

title: Spee's Cautio Criminalis
blurb: The Jesuit Friedrich Spee, having confessed condemned 'witches,' anonymously attacked the use of torture — an early, courageous voice against the trials.
source: Clark, Thinking with Demons

title: The Royal Society and the retreat of magic
blurb: The new experimental philosophy set out to distinguish itself from magic — even as many founders (Boyle, Newton) still practised alchemy in private. Magic became the discipline's disowned parent.
source: Keith Thomas, Religion and the Decline of Magic

title: Isobel Gowdie's astonishing confession
blurb: Without evident torture, the Scottish woman Isobel Gowdie confessed to fairies, flight, and a covenant with the Devil in vivid detail — a unique window into what a 'witch' believed she did.
source: Emma Wilby, The Visions of Isobel Gowdie

title: Janet Horne, last executed for witchcraft in Britain
blurb: The burning of Janet Horne in Sutherland was Britain's last legal witch-execution — the panic guttering out a decade before the law itself was repealed.
source: Ronald Hutton, The Triumph of the Moon

title: Anna Göldi, 'the last witch' of Europe
blurb: Executed in Glarus, Anna Göldi is often called Europe's last witch — a judicial killing so late it embarrassed the Enlightenment that produced it.
source: historical record

title: Mathers translates the Goetia
blurb: S. L. MacGregor Mathers and the young Aleister Crowley put the Ars Goetia — the seventy-two demons of the Lemegeton — into the English that would seed a century of pop occultism.
mtg: The seals and demons that fantasy 'infernal pact' cards still echo.
source: Joseph Peterson (ed.), Lesser Key of Solomon (context)

title: Ginzburg's The Night Battles
blurb: Carlo Ginzburg uncovered the benandanti — Friulian peasants who believed they fought witches in dream-battles for the harvest — showing a folk-magic reality beneath the trial records.
source: Carlo Ginzburg, The Night Battles

title: Yates' The Rosicrucian Enlightenment
blurb: Frances Yates extended her Hermetic thesis into the seventeenth century, arguing a hidden magical current ran through the birth of modern science and the Rosicrucian furore.
source: Frances Yates, The Rosicrucian Enlightenment

title: Cohn's Europe's Inner Demons
blurb: Norman Cohn dismantled the myth of a real witch-cult, showing the sabbat as a fantasy assembled by inquisitors — the corrective to Murray that reshaped the field.
source: Norman Cohn, Europe's Inner Demons

title: The Malleus meets modern scholarship
blurb: A wave of social-historical work (Thomas, Macfarlane, Cohn, Ginzburg) turned the witch trials from a curiosity into a central problem of early-modern history — the scholarship this project leans on.
source: renaissance magic corpus

title: Hutton's The Triumph of the Moon
blurb: Ronald Hutton wrote the first rigorous history of modern Pagan witchcraft, tracing Wicca's real, recent invention from Romantic, Masonic, and folkloric threads.
source: Ronald Hutton, The Triumph of the Moon

title: Owen Davies' Grimoires and the popular book of magic
blurb: Scholarship on cheap-print magic (Davies) showed the grimoire never died — it went mass-market, from the Petit Albert to the internet PDF.
source: Owen Davies, Grimoires: A History

title: The digital-humanities study of magic
blurb: Databases, corpora, and text-mining opened a new way to study historical magic at scale — the tradition this very project belongs to, turning Ted's libraries into queryable knowledge.
mtg: The method behind this site's Word Histories and this timeline.
source: this project; docs/RESEARCH_METHOD.md
